metadata.title: Why the Tumor Genome Is a Fossil Record of What the Cell Encountered
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=preserved pattern/correct signature/intact repair, CRIMSON=DNA damage/adduct/broken sequence
metadata.note: Exclusions: no full chemical mechanism for all carcinogens (only benzo[a]pyrene and UV in detail); no COSMIC signature database details; no procarcinogen activation enzyme kinetics; no aflatoxin-HBV interaction (separate card); no linear no-threshold model for radiation.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Wonder register (voices/wonder/VOICE.md + MEDHAVY.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the MEDHAVY outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Medhavy.com
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A sequencing report lands in 2025. It accurately names what was inside a lung cell in 1985.
beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] Why the Tumor Genome Is a Fossil Record

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] Why the Tumor Genome Is a Fossil Record

beats.narration_text: An oncologist reads: 340 mutations, dominant pattern G-to-T transversions at specific trinucleotide contexts. She recognizes the exposure before opening the clinical history.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] sequencing report panel with G->T dominant pattern, Signature 4 chip

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [GRAPHIC] sequencing report panel with G->T dominant pattern, Signature 4 chip

beats.narration_text: Each carcinogen leaves a distinctive mutation pattern that persists for decades. Here is why that happens. How does a chemical write a permanent fingerprint into DNA?
beats.new_visual_element: [QUESTION] gap formula on screen: pattern persists decades -- why?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [QUESTION] gap formula on screen: pattern persists decades -- why?

beats.narration_text: Mutations are heritable changes in the DNA sequence. When a cell divides, every daughter cell inherits exactly the same altered letter — locked in, copied forward.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] cell division tree: G->T mutation inherited across generations

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [GRAPHIC] cell division tree: G->T mutation inherited across generations

beats.narration_text: A chemical hits one cell once. You might expect one random mutation — lost in millions of normal letters, invisible in the noise. That is not what we find.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] genome grid: one crimson G among teal letters

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [GRAPHIC] genome grid: one crimson G among teal letters

beats.narration_text: Here's what's actually happening with benzo-a-pyrene. It does not hit randomly. Enzymes convert it to BPDE — which attacks guanine specifically, at specific trinucleotide motifs. The chemistry is not random.

props.headline: But benzo-a-pyrene doesn't hit randomly. Enzymes convert it to BPDE --…

beats.new_visual_element: [STILL geo] benzo[a]pyrene -> BPDE -> guanine adduct -> G->T chain

beats.narration_text: The BPDE adduct distorts the helix. DNA polymerase cannot read through it accurately. It inserts adenine opposite the damaged guanine — a predictable error, not a random one.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] DNA ladder: adduct on G, kinked rung, polymerase inserts A (wrong)

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [GRAPHIC] DNA ladder: adduct on G, kinked rung, polymerase inserts A (wrong)

beats.narration_text: After one more replication round, that mismatch becomes a fixed G-to-T transversion — permanent, heritable, copied into every daughter cell from that point forward.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two daughter cells: one G:C (teal), one T:A (crimson, fixed mutation)

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [GRAPHIC] two daughter cells: one G:C (teal), one T:A (crimson, fixed mutation)

beats.narration_text: UV works differently, and the contrast is instructive. A photon fuses adjacent pyrimidines into a cyclobutane dimer. Polymerase inserts adenine opposite both — leaving C-to-T transitions at dipyrimidine sites. Different chemistry, different fingerprint.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] UV dimer formation: C-C + photon -> C=C dimer -> C->T transition

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [GRAPHIC] UV dimer formation: C-C + photon -> C=C dimer -> C->T transition

beats.narration_text: Decades of exposure mean thousands of these context-specific errors. The pattern accumulates. The chemical fingerprint grows stronger with every pack-year, every hour of sun — until it is unmistakable.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] bar chart growing: G->T bars at GpC context rising over time

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [GRAPHIC] bar chart growing: G->T bars at GpC context rising over time

beats.narration_text: Four carcinogens, four fingerprints — each one distinct. Tobacco: G-to-T transversions. UV: C-to-T at dipyrimidines. Aflatoxin: one sharp spike at TP53 codon 249. MMR deficiency: hundreds of mutations, evenly spread.

props.headline: Four carcinogens, four fingerprints. Tobacco: G-to-T. UV: C-to-T at dipyrimidines. Aflatoxin:…

beats.new_visual_element: [STILL geo] four mutational signature bar charts side-by-side

beats.narration_text: A lung cancer in a never-smoker that carries the tobacco signature reveals a secondary exposure the clinical history missed. The genome turns out to be a more reliable witness than memory.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two-column: genome says tobacco signature / file says never-smoker

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [GRAPHIC] two-column: genome says tobacco signature / file says never-smoker

beats.narration_text: Consider four tumor genomes from four different patients. The patterns will name the exposure before you open the clinical file.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] four empty genome boxes -- illustrative example setup

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [GRAPHIC] four empty genome boxes -- illustrative example setup

beats.narration_text: Melanoma: C-to-T, UV. Lung adenocarcinoma: G-to-T, tobacco. Liver tumor: codon 249 spike, aflatoxin. Colon cancer: 400 mutations, uniform — MMR deficiency. Four histories. Illustrative.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] four illustrated tumor genome cards with pattern and carcinogen labels (illustrative)

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [GRAPHIC] four illustrated tumor genome cards with pattern and carcinogen labels (illustrative)

beats.narration_text: Here's why this works. Each carcinogen makes adducts at specific sequence contexts. Polymerase misreads them with context-specific bias. The pattern is fixed, heritable — a chemical fingerprint written into the DNA, waiting to be read.
beats.new_visual_element: [ENDCARD] CANCER BIOLOGY eyebrow, recap: fingerprint in DNA

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [ENDCARD] CANCER BIOLOGY eyebrow, recap: fingerprint in DNA
